title: Trump: 'NATO Did Not Support Us — and It Will Not Support Us in the Future'; Stoltenberg Warns Alliance 'Not Written in Stone'
detail: In his sharpest public statement on NATO to date, President Trump declared: 'NATO did not support us — and it will not support us in the future!' The statement — flagged by CNN in an April 15 fact-check of Trump's claims on NATO, taxes, and immigration — represented a historic escalation beyond the alliance's previous crisis level. Trump's framing moved from conditional ('why would we be there for them?') to a flat declarative rejection of future alliance solidarity. Former NATO Secretary General Jens Stoltenberg, in a widely-circulated response, issued a stark warning: 'It's not a natural law that we will have NATO forever. It's not written in stone that NATO will exist for the next ten years.' Stoltenberg urged NATO allies and the US Senate to take Trump's threats seriously and resist normalizing alliance conditionality. The US Senate's bipartisan NATO Observer Group issued a joint statement by Co-Chairs opposing any US withdrawal from NATO and affirming the alliance's strategic value. CNN's fact-check noted Trump's claim that NATO 'wasn't there' during the Iran war misrepresented allied contributions: all 32 members had provided basing rights, logistics, or overflight access — the specific items Rutte had cited as concrete allied support.

media.caption: Trump declares 'NATO will not support us in the future' as Stoltenberg warns the alliance is not guaranteed to exist

title: WSJ: Europe Drafts NATO Contingency Plan for US Withdrawal — Rutte Chairs UDCG Berlin; Magyar Nominated as Hungarian PM
detail: The Wall Street Journal reported that European NATO members are actively drafting contingency plans for operating without US support — including promoting more Europeans to senior NATO command positions and replacing American military resources with European assets in areas such as logistics, air defense, and intelligence coordination. The plans represent the most concrete organizational response to Trump's escalating NATO threats, indicating European capitals no longer view US withdrawal as a theoretical risk but a scenario requiring operational planning. Separately, NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte chaired the Ukraine Defence Contact Group meeting in Berlin on April 15, welcoming additional allied aid packages for Ukraine and reiterating that European defense investment strengthened rather than replaced the transatlantic alliance. In Budapest, Hungarian President Tamás Sulyok formally nominated Péter Magyar as Prime Minister following Tisza's landslide election victory — a historic transition that removes NATO's most obstructionist internal voice and potentially shifts alliance consensus ahead of the Helsingborg Foreign Ministers summit (May 21–22).

media.caption: Rutte chairs Ukraine Defence Contact Group in Berlin as Europe accelerates autonomous defense planning
